text: Pulmonary hypertension is defined by a mean pulmonary arterial pressure (mPAP) > 20 mmHg at rest (Table 5). This is supported by studies assessing the upper limit of normal pulmonary arterial pressure (PAP) in healthy subjects, and by studies investigating the prognostic relevance of increased PAP.

It is essential to include PVR and pulmonary arterial wedge pressure (PAWP) in the definition of pre-capillary PH, in order to discriminate elevated PAP due to pulmonary vascular disease (PVD) from that due to left heart disease (LHD), elevated pulmonary blood flow, or increased intrathoracic pressure. Based on the available data, the upper limit of normal PVR and the lowest prognostically relevant threshold of PVR is approximately 2 Wood units (WU).

Although the upper limit of normal PAWP is considered to be 12 mmHg, previous ESC/ERS Guidelines and the recent ESC Heart Failure Association consensus recommendation suggest a higher threshold for the invasive diagnosis of HFpEF (PAWP ≥ 15 mmHg). Almost all therapeutic studies of PAH have used the PAWP ≤ 15 mmHg threshold, so it is recommended to keep PAWP ≤ 15 mmHg as the threshold for pre-capillary PH.

Table 5 — Haemodynamic definitions of pulmonary hypertension. PH: mPAP > 20 mmHg. Pre-capillary PH: mPAP > 20 mmHg, PAWP ≤ 15 mmHg, and PVR > 2 WU. Isolated post-capillary PH (IpcPH): mPAP > 20 mmHg, PAWP > 15 mmHg, and PVR ≤ 2 WU. Combined post- and pre-capillary PH (CpcPH): mPAP > 20 mmHg, PAWP > 15 mmHg, and PVR > 2 WU. Exercise PH: mPAP/CO slope between rest and exercise > 3 mmHg/L/min.

Patients with PAH are haemodynamically characterized by pre-capillary PH in the absence of other causes of pre-capillary PH, such as CTEPH and PH associated with lung diseases. All PH groups may comprise both pre- and post-capillary components contributing to PAP elevation, and the primary classification should be based on the presumed predominant cause of the pulmonary pressure increase.

Post-capillary PH is haemodynamically defined as mPAP > 20 mmHg and PAWP > 15 mmHg. Pulmonary vascular resistance is used to differentiate between patients with post-capillary PH who have a significant pre-capillary component (PVR > 2 WU — combined post- and pre-capillary PH [CpcPH]) and those who do not (PVR ≤ 2 WU — isolated post-capillary PH [IpcPH]).

There are patients with elevated mPAP (> 20 mmHg) but low PVR (≤ 2 WU) and low PAWP (≤ 15 mmHg). These patients are frequently characterized by elevated pulmonary blood flow and, although they have PH, they do not fulfil the criteria of pre- or post-capillary PH; this haemodynamic condition may be described by the term 'unclassified PH'.

Exercise PH, defined by an mPAP/cardiac output (CO) slope > 3 mmHg/L/min between rest and exercise, has been re-introduced. An mPAP/CO slope > 3 mmHg/L/min is not physiological in subjects aged < 60 years and may rarely be present in healthy subjects aged > 60 years. An increased mPAP/CO slope defines an abnormal haemodynamic response to exercise but does not allow differentiation between pre- and post-capillary causes; the PAWP/CO slope with a threshold > 2 mmHg/L/min may best differentiate between pre- and post-capillary causes of exercise PH.

Table 6 — Clinical classification of pulmonary hypertension, Group 1 Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH): 1.1 Idiopathic (1.1.1 non-responders and 1.1.2 acute responders at vasoreactivity testing); 1.2 Heritable; 1.3 Associated with drugs and toxins; 1.4 Associated with connective tissue disease, HIV infection, portal hypertension, congenital heart disease, or schistosomiasis; 1.5 PAH with features of venous/capillary (PVOD/PCH) involvement; 1.6 Persistent PH of the newborn.

Table 6 — Group 2 PH associated with left heart disease: 2.1 Heart failure (2.1.1 with preserved ejection fraction; 2.1.2 with reduced or mildly reduced ejection fraction); 2.2 Valvular heart disease; 2.3 Congenital/acquired cardiovascular conditions leading to post-capillary PH. Left ventricular ejection fraction for HF with reduced ejection fraction is ≤ 40%; for HF with mildly reduced ejection fraction it is 41–49%.

Table 6 — Group 3 PH associated with lung diseases and/or hypoxia: 3.1 Obstructive lung disease or emphysema; 3.2 Restrictive lung disease; 3.3 Lung disease with mixed restrictive/obstructive pattern; 3.4 Hypoventilation syndromes; 3.5 Hypoxia without lung disease (e.g. high altitude); 3.6 Developmental lung disorders.

Table 6 — Group 4 PH associated with pulmonary artery obstructions: 4.1 Chronic thrombo-embolic PH; 4.2 Other pulmonary artery obstructions. Group 5 PH with unclear and/or multifactorial mechanisms: 5.1 Haematological disorders; 5.2 Systemic disorders; 5.3 Metabolic disorders; 5.4 Chronic renal failure with or without haemodialysis; 5.5 Pulmonary tumour thrombotic microangiopathy; 5.6 Fibrosing mediastinitis.

Globally, left heart disease (LHD) is the leading cause of PH. Lung disease, especially chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), is the second most common cause. Prevalence is higher in individuals aged > 65 years due to the presence of cardiac and pulmonary causes of PH.

Typical ECG abnormalities in PH include: P pulmonale (P > 0.25 mV in lead II); right or sagittal axis deviation (QRS axis > 90° or indeterminable); RV hypertrophy (R/S > 1, with R > 0.5 mV in V1; R in V1 + S in lead V5 > 1 mV); right bundle branch block—complete or incomplete (qR or rSR patterns in V1); RV strain pattern (ST depression/T-wave inversion in the right pre-cordial V1–4 and inferior II, III, aVF leads); and a prolonged QTc interval (unspecific).

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Radiographic signs of pulmonary hypertension and concomitant abnormalities include right heart enlargement, PA enlargement (including aneurysmal dilatation), and pruning of the peripheral vessels. Signs of left heart disease/pulmonary congestion include central air space opacification, interlobular septal thickening (‘Kerley B’ lines), pleural effusions, a ‘water-bottle’ shape of the cardiac silhouette, left atrial enlargement (including splayed carina), and left ventricular dilation. Signs of lung disease include flattening of the diaphragm and hyperlucency (COPD/emphysema), lung volume loss, and reticular opacification (fibrotic lung disease).

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: These guidelines recommend using the peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity (TRV) (and not the estimated sPAP) as the key variable for assigning the echocardiographic probability of PH. A peak TRV > 2.8 m/s may suggest PH; however, the presence or absence of PH cannot be reliably determined by TRV alone. Lowering the TRV threshold in view of the revised haemodynamic definition of PH is not supported by available data.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Echocardiographic measures of RV function include the tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE), RV fractional area change (RV-FAC), RV free-wall strain, and tricuspid annulus velocity (S′ wave) derived from tissue Doppler imaging, and potentially RV ejection fraction (RVEF) derived from 3D echocardiography. Furthermore, the TAPSE/sPAP ratio—representing a non-invasive measure of RV–PA coupling—may aid in diagnosing PH. The pattern of RV outflow tract (RVOT) blood flow (mid-systolic ‘notching’) may suggest pre-capillary PH.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Transthoracic echocardiographic parameters in the assessment of pulmonary hypertension include an enlarged right ventricle, a dilated RV with basal RV/LV ratio > 1.0 (four-chamber view), a flattened interventricular septum (arrows) leading to a ‘D-shaped’ LV with decreased LV eccentricity index, and a distended inferior vena cava with diminished inspiratory collapsibility. An RVOT acceleration time of pulmonary ejection < 105 ms with a mid-systolic ‘notch’ is indicative of pre-capillary PH, and RV fractional area change is reduced (< 35%).

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Additional transthoracic echocardiographic signs of pulmonary hypertension include a decreased tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion (TAPSE) measured with M-Mode (< 18 mm), a decreased peak systolic (S′) velocity of the tricuspid annulus (< 9.5 cm/s) measured with tissue Doppler, an enlarged right atrial area (> 18 cm2) in the four-chamber view, and an increased systolic peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity (peak TRV) measured with continuous wave Doppler. Systolic pulmonary artery pressure (sPAP) is estimated as sPAP = TR pressure gradient + estimated RAP.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: The CT signs suggesting the presence of PH include an enlarged PA diameter, a PA-to-aorta ratio > 0.9, and enlarged right heart chambers. A combination of three parameters (PA diameter ≥ 30 mm, RVOT wall thickness ≥ 6 mm, and septal deviation ≥ 140° [or RV:LV ratio ≥ 1]) is highly predictive of PH. Non-contrast chest CT can help determine the cause of PH when there are features of parenchymal lung disease, and may also point towards the presence of PVOD/PCH by showing centrilobular ground-glass opacities, septal lines, and lymphadenopathy.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Additional echocardiographic signs suggestive of pulmonary hypertension — ventricles category: RV/LV basal diameter/area ratio > 1.0; flattening of the interventricular septum (LVEI > 1.1 in systole and/or diastole); and TAPSE/sPAP ratio < 0.55 mm/mmHg. Pulmonary artery category: RVOT AT < 105 ms and/or mid-systolic notching; early diastolic pulmonary regurgitation velocity > 2.2 m/s; PA diameter > AR (aortic root) diameter; and PA diameter > 25 mm.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Additional echocardiographic signs suggestive of pulmonary hypertension — inferior vena cava and RA category: IVC diameter > 21 mm with decreased inspiratory collapse (< 50% with a sniff or < 20% with quiet inspiration); and RA area (end-systole) > 18 cm2. Signs from at least two categories (A/B/C) must be present to alter the level of echocardiographic probability of PH.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: The echocardiographic probability of pulmonary hypertension is based on peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity and the presence of other echo PH signs. A peak TRV ≤ 2.8 m/s (or unmeasurable) with no other PH signs indicates LOW probability; a peak TRV ≤ 2.8 m/s with other signs, or a peak TRV of 2.9–3.4 m/s, indicates INTERMEDIATE probability; and a peak TRV > 3.4 m/s indicates HIGH probability. The TRV threshold of 2.8 m/s was not changed according to the updated haemodynamic definition of PH.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for diagnosing and classifying PH. Haemodynamic measures obtained during RHC and their normal values are: mean right atrial pressure (RAP) 2–6 mmHg; systolic PA pressure (sPAP) 15–30 mmHg; diastolic PA pressure (dPAP) 4–12 mmHg; mean PA pressure (mPAP) 8–20 mmHg; mean pulmonary arterial wedge pressure (PAWP) ≤ 15 mmHg; cardiac output (CO) 4–8 L/min; mixed venous oxygen saturation (SvO2) 65–80%; and arterial oxygen saturation (SaO2) 95–100%.

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: Calculated haemodynamic parameters obtained during right heart catheterization and their normal values are: pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) 0.3–2.0 WU; pulmonary vascular resistance index (PVRI) 3–3.5 WU·m2; total pulmonary resistance (TPR) < 3 WU; cardiac index (CI) 2.5–4.0 L/min·m2; stroke volume (SV) 60–100 mL; stroke volume index (SVI) 33–47 mL/m2; and pulmonary arterial compliance (PAC) > 2.3 mL/mmHg. PVR = (mPAP − PAWP)/CO; TPR = mPAP/CO; PAC = SV/(sPAP − dPAP).

metadata.section: Echo / CT / ECG signs & RHC normals

text: A multistep, pragmatic approach to diagnosis should be considered in patients with unexplained dyspnoea or symptoms/signs raising suspicion of PH. This strategy is depicted in detail in Figure 6 and Table 14. The diagnostic algorithm does not address screening for specific groups at risk of PH.

Step 1 (suspicion): Patients with PH are likely to be seen by first-line physicians, mainly general practitioners, for non-specific symptoms. Initial evaluation should include a comprehensive medical (including familial) history, thorough physical examination (including measurement of blood pressure, heart rate, and pulse oximetry), blood test to determine BNP/NT-proBNP, and resting ECG. This first step may raise a suspicion of a cardiac or respiratory disorder causing the symptoms.

Step 2 (detection): The second step includes classical, non-invasive lung and cardiac testing. Among those tests, echocardiography is an important step in the diagnostic algorithm, as it assigns a level of probability of PH, irrespective of the cause. In addition, it is an important step in identifying other cardiac disorders.
Based on this initial assessment, if causes other than PH are identified and/or in case of low probability of PH, patients should be managed accordingly.

Step 3 (confirmation): Patients should be referred to a PH centre for further evaluation in the following situations: (1) when an intermediate/high probability of PH is established; (2) in the presence of risk factors for PAH, or a history of PE. A comprehensive work-up should be performed, with the goal of establishing the differential diagnoses and distinguishing between the various causes of PH according to the current clinical classification. The PH centre is responsible for performing an invasive assessment according to the clinical scenario.

Fast-track referral at any time in case of warning signs, or when PAH or CTEPH are suspected: refer to PH centre (Class I) for a comprehensive PH work-up (Table 14 and Recommendation Table 2) and invasive assessment as needed. Warning signs include rapid progression of symptoms, severely reduced exercise capacity, pre-syncope or syncope on mild exertion, and signs of right heart failure. For chest CT as indicated, CT pulmonary angiography is recommended if PH is suspected.

Lung assessment by specialist includes pulmonary function tests (PFT), arterial blood gas (ABG), chest X-ray, chest CT, and cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET). Risk factors for PAH include connective tissue disease (especially systemic sclerosis), portal hypertension, HIV infection, and family history of PAH. Risk factors for CTEPH include history of PE, permanent intravascular devices, inflammatory bowel diseases, essential thrombocythaemia, splenectomy, high-dose thyroid hormone replacement, and malignancy.

At any time, warning signs must be recognized, as they are associated with worse outcomes and warrant immediate intervention. Such warning signs include rapidly evolving or severe symptoms (WHO-FC III/IV), clinical signs of RV failure, syncope, signs of low CO state, poorly tolerated arrhythmias, and compromised or deteriorated haemodynamic status (hypotension, tachycardia). The presence of RV dysfunction by echocardiography, elevated levels of cardiac biomarkers, and/or haemodynamic instability must prompt referral to a PH centre for immediate assessment.

Echocardiography is recommended as the first-line, non-invasive, diagnostic investigation in suspected PH (Class I, Level B). It is recommended to assign an echocardiographic probability of PH, based on an abnormal TRV and the presence of other echocardiographic signs suggestive of PH (Class I, Level B). It is recommended to maintain the current threshold for TRV (> 2.8 m/s) for echocardiographic probability of PH according to the updated haemodynamic definition (Class I, Level C).

Based on the probability of PH by echocardiography, further testing should be considered in the clinical context (i.e. symptoms and risk factors or associated conditions for PAH/CTEPH) (Class IIa, Level B). In symptomatic patients with intermediate echocardiographic probability of PH, CPET may be considered to further determine the likelihood of PH (Class IIb, Level C).

Ventilation/perfusion or perfusion lung scan is recommended in patients with unexplained PH to assess for CTEPH (Class I, Level C). CT pulmonary angiography is recommended in the work-up of patients with suspected CTEPH (Class I, Level C). Chest CT should be considered in all patients with PH (Class IIa, Level C). Pulmonary function tests with DLCO are recommended in the initial evaluation of patients with PH (Class I, Level C).

Characteristic diagnostic features by group (Table 14). Group 1 (PAH): variable age, but young female patients may be predominantly affected; chest radiography shows RA/RV/PA enlargement and pruning of peripheral vessels; spirometry/PFT normal or mildly impaired; DLCO normal or mild-to-moderately reduced (low DLCO in SSc-PAH, PVOD, and some IPAH phenotypes); V/Q normal or matched; right heart catheterization shows pre-capillary PH.

Group 2 (PH associated with left heart disease): mostly elderly patients, female predominance in case of HFpEF; history and clinical findings suggestive of LHD; chest radiography shows LA/LV enlargement, cardiomegaly, and occasional signs of congestion; echocardiography shows signs of LHD (HFrEF, HFpEF, valvular); right heart catheterization shows post-capillary PH. Group 4 (PH associated with pulmonary artery obstructions): history of VTE; V/Q shows mismatched perfusion defect; chest CT shows intravascular filling defects, mosaic perfusion, and enlarged bronchial arteries; RHC shows pre- (or post-) capillary PH.

Group 3 (PH associated with lung disease): mostly elderly patients, male predominance; history and clinical findings suggestive of lung disease, smoking history common; oxygen requirement common, often profound hypoxaemia in severe PH; spirometry/PFT abnormal as determined by the underlying lung disease; DLCO often very low (< 45% predicted); chest radiography and chest CT show signs of parenchymal lung disease; right heart catheterization shows pre-capillary PH.

World Health Organization functional class (WHO-FC) is assessed both at diagnosis and follow-up, and worsening WHO-FC is one of the most alarming indicators of disease progression, which should trigger further investigations to identify the cause(s) of clinical deterioration. WHO-FC I: patients with PH but without resulting limitation of physical activity; ordinary physical activity does not cause undue dyspnoea or fatigue, chest pain, or near syncope. WHO-FC IV: patients with PH with an inability to carry out any physical activity without symptoms; these patients manifest signs of right HF, and dyspnoea and/or fatigue may even be present at rest.

Currently available risk-stratification tools include haemodynamic variables for prognostication: RAP and PVR in REVEAL risk scores, and RAP, CI, and SvO2 in the ESC/ERS risk-stratification table. The mPAP provides little prognostic information, except in acute vasodilator responders. A recent study from France, which combined clinical and haemodynamic parameters, found that WHO-FC, 6MWD, RAP, and SVI (but not SV and SvO2) were independent predictors of outcome.

To refine the risk-stratification table (Table 16), SVI criteria are now added with the cut-off values of > 38 mL/m2 and < 31 mL/m2 to determine low-risk and high-risk status, respectively. Estimated sPAP at rest is not prognostic and irrelevant to therapeutic decision-making; an increase in sPAP does not necessarily reflect disease progression and a decrease in sPAP does not necessarily reflect improvement.

In the Table 16 three-strata model of comprehensive risk assessment in pulmonary arterial hypertension, low risk corresponds to estimated 1-year mortality < 5%, intermediate risk to 5-20%, and high risk to > 20%. Signs of right HF are absent in low and intermediate risk and present in high risk; progression of symptoms is no progression (low), slow (intermediate), or rapid (high); syncope is none (low), occasional syncope (intermediate), or repeated syncope (high); WHO-FC is I or II (low), III (intermediate), or IV (high).

In the Table 16 three-strata model, 6MWD is > 440 m for low risk, 165-440 m for intermediate risk, and < 165 m for high risk. On CPET, peak VO2 is > 15 mL/min/kg (> 65% pred.) for low risk, 11-15 mL/min/kg (35-65% pred.) for intermediate risk, and < 11 mL/min/kg (< 35% pred.) for high risk; the VE/VCO2 slope is < 36 (low), 36-44 (intermediate), and > 44 (high).

In the Table 16 three-strata model, biomarkers BNP and NT-proBNP stratify risk: BNP < 50 ng/L and NT-proBNP < 300 ng/L for low risk; BNP 50-800 ng/L and NT-proBNP 300-1100 ng/L for intermediate risk; BNP > 800 ng/L and NT-proBNP > 1100 ng/L for high risk.

In the Table 16 three-strata model, echocardiographic and cMRI variables stratify risk: RA area < 18 cm2 (low), 18-26 cm2 (intermediate), > 26 cm2 (high); TAPSE/sPAP > 0.32 mm/mmHg (low), 0.19-0.32 mm/mmHg (intermediate), < 0.19 mm/mmHg (high); no pericardial effusion (low), minimal pericardial effusion (intermediate), moderate or large pericardial effusion (high); RVEF > 54% (low), 37-54% (intermediate), < 37% (high); SVI > 40 mL/m2 (low), 26-40 mL/m2 (intermediate), < 26 mL/m2 (high); RVESVI < 42 mL/m2 (low), 42-54 mL/m2 (intermediate), > 54 mL/m2 (high).

In the Table 16 three-strata model, haemodynamic variables stratify risk: RAP < 8 mmHg (low), 8-14 mmHg (intermediate), > 14 mmHg (high); CI 2.5 L/min/m2 (low), 2.0-2.4 L/min/m2 (intermediate), < 2.0 L/min/m2 (high); SVI > 38 mL/m2 (low), 31-38 mL/m2 (intermediate), < 31 mL/m2 (high); SvO2 > 65% (low), 60-65% (intermediate), < 60% (high).

The 6-minute walking test (6MWT) is the most widely used measure of exercise capacity in PH centres, and the 6MWD is influenced by factors such as sex, age, height, weight, comorbidities, need for oxygen, learning curve, and motivation. A recent investigation showed that the best absolute-threshold values for 1-year mortality and 1-year survival, respectively, were 165 m and 440 m.

Refined cut-off values for BNP (< 50, 50-199, 200-800, and > 800 ng/L) and NT-proBNP (< 300, 300-649, 650-1100, and > 1100 ng/L) for low, intermediate-low, intermediate-high, and high risk, respectively, have recently been introduced as part of a four-strata risk-assessment strategy. BNP and NT-proBNP remain the only biomarkers routinely used in clinical practice at PH centres, correlating with myocardial stress and providing prognostic information, but they are not specific for PH as they can be elevated in other forms of heart disease.

Table 18 lists the variables used to calculate the simplified four-strata risk-assessment tool, with points assigned 1 (low risk), 2 (intermediate-low risk), 3 (intermediate-high risk), and 4 (high risk). WHO-FC is I or II (1 point), III (3 points), or IV (4 points); 6MWD is > 440 m, 320-440 m, 165-319 m, or < 165 m; BNP is < 50, 50-199, 200-800, or > 800 ng/L; NT-proBNP is < 300, 300-649, 650-1100, or > 1100 ng/L. Risk is calculated by dividing the sum of all grades by the number of variables and rounding to the next integer.

The observed 1-year mortality rates in the four risk strata were 0-3%, 2-7%, 9-19%, and > 20%, respectively. The main advantage of the four-strata model over the three-strata model is better discrimination within the intermediate-risk group, which helps guide therapeutic decision-making. However, the three-strata model is maintained for initial assessment, which should be comprehensive and include echocardiographic and haemodynamic variables.

For risk stratification at diagnosis, use of the three-strata model is recommended taking into account as many factors as possible (Table 16), with a strong emphasis on disease type, WHO-FC, 6MWD, BNP/NT-proBNP, and haemodynamics. Several studies have identified WHO-FC, 6MWD, and BNP/NT-proBNP as the strongest prognostic predictors, and it is recommended to use at least these three variables for risk stratification.

Pulmonary hypertension is frequently observed in patients with COPD and/or emphysema, ILD, combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema (CPFE), and hypoventilation syndromes. Pulmonary hypertension is uncommon in obstructive sleep apnoea unless other conditions coexist, such as COPD or daytime hypoventilation.

In patients with lung disease, PH is categorized as non-severe or severe, depending on haemodynamic findings. In the 2015 ESC/ERS Guidelines for the diagnosis and treatment of PH, severe PH was defined by mPAP > 35 mmHg or mPAP ≥ 25 mmHg with CI < 2.5 L/min/m2. However, two recent studies have demonstrated that a PVR > 5 WU is a better threshold for predicting worse prognosis in patients with PH associated with both COPD and ILD.
Based on these data, the current guidelines used PVR to distinguish between non-severe PH (PVR ≤ 5 WU) and severe PH (PVR > 5 WU).

Whereas non-severe PH is common in advanced COPD and ILD defined by spirometric criteria, severe PH is uncommon, occurring in 1–5% of cases of COPD and < 10% of patients with advanced ILD, with limited data in obesity hypoventilation syndrome. Even non-severe PH in lung disease negatively impacts symptoms and survival, and is associated with increased hospitalization.

Patients with lung disease and severe PH have a worse outcome than those with non-severe PH, providing evidence that this distinction has clinical significance. It is noteworthy that developing severe PH is largely independent of spirometry but usually accompanied by hypoxaemia, low PaCO2, and a significant reduction in DLCO.

A number of distinct phenotypes of PH in patients with lung disease, including a pulmonary vascular phenotype, have been proposed. The pulmonary vascular phenotype is characterized by better preserved spirometry, low DLCO, hypoxaemia, a range of parenchymal involvement on lung imaging, and a circulatory limitation to exercise.

Recent studies have shown that the clinical characteristics, disease trajectory, response to treatment, and histological correlates of patients with severe PH and minor lung disease are different to those in patients with IPAH, including a poorer prognosis.

Pathophysiology of pulmonary hypertension associated with lung disease (group 3) involves remodelling of the pulmonary vessels, including vascular pruning, in addition to remodelling of airways and parenchyma. As the contribution of vascular remodelling and hypoxaemia increases, PH progresses from no PH to non-severe PH to severe PH (PVR > 5 WU), with exercise limitation shifting from mostly ventilatory to mostly circulatory.

In patients with lung disease, symptoms of PH, especially exertional dyspnoea, overlap with those of the underlying condition. Physical findings may also be non-specific; for example, ankle swelling is common during episodes of ventilatory failure in COPD, where activation of the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system may cause fluid retention, usually in the setting of preserved RV function.

Non-invasive tests—such as ECG showing right axis deviation or RV strain, elevated levels of BNP/NT-proBNP, CPET, or features on cross-sectional imaging—may suggest the diagnosis of PH in patients with lung disease. Echocardiography remains the most widely used non-invasive diagnostic tool for assessing PH; however, the accuracy of echocardiography in patients with advanced respiratory diseases is low, with a TRV unmeasurable in > 50% of patients in some studies, and there is a tendency to overestimate PAP and misclassify patients with PH.

More recent data suggest that a stepwise, composite, echocardiographic score can identify patients with severe PH, with and without an estimate of TRV, using other echocardiographic features including RA area, RV:LV ratio, and LV eccentricity index. Where PH is suspected, combining echocardiography with a contrast-enhanced CT may aid diagnostic assessment and disease classification. Pulmonary artery enlargement, RV outflow hypertrophy, and increased RV:LV ratio may suggest a diagnosis of PH.

Key parts of evaluating suspected PH in lung disease include integrating: (1) the presence or absence of risk factors for PAH, CTEPH, or LHD; (2) clinical features, including disease trajectory and oxygen requirements; (3) PFTs, including DLCO and blood gas analysis; (4) NT-proBNP measurements, ECG, and echocardiography; and (5) cross-sectional imaging with contrast-enhanced CT, SPECT, or V/Q lung scan and, in selected cases, cMRI to assess the need for RHC.

All patients whose symptoms can be attributed to post-thrombo-embolic fibrotic obstructions within the pulmonary artery are considered to have CTEPD with or without PH; CTEPH remains the preferred term for patients with PH.

Chronic thrombo-embolic pulmonary disease describes symptomatic patients with mismatched perfusion defects on V/Q scan and with signs of chronic, organized, fibrotic clots on CTPA or DSA, such as ring-like stenoses, webs/slits, and chronic total occlusions (pouch lesions or tapered lesions), after at least 3 months of therapeutic anticoagulation.
Pulmonary hypertension in this setting is not only a consequence of pulmonary artery obstruction by organized fibrotic clots but can also be related to the associated microvasculopathy.
In those patients without PH at rest, breathlessness could be due to exercise PH and/or increased dead space ventilation.

Excluding ventilatory limitation, deconditioning and psychogenic hyperventilation syndrome by CPET and LV myocardial or valvular disease by echocardiography is of utmost importance when making therapeutic decisions in patients with CTEPD without PH.

Chronic thrombo-embolic pulmonary hypertension is a common and important cause of PH, with a distinct management strategy. Thus, the possibility of CTEPH should be carefully considered in all patients with PH.

In the CTEPH diagnostic algorithm for symptomatic patients, CTEPH is suspected from a history of pulmonary embolism or risk factors for CTEPH, and perfusion imaging by V/Q scan (or a new modality) is performed to detect any mismatched perfusion defects.

When mismatched perfusion defects are present, echocardiography is used; intermediate or high probability of PH on echocardiography prompts referral to a CTEPH/PH centre for comprehensive work-up, including CTPA plus DSA when CTPA is inconclusive, and RHC.
Treatment assessment by a multidisciplinary team at a CTEPH centre is recommended (Class I) for patients with confirmed CTEPH.
Alternative perfusion imaging techniques such as iodine subtraction mapping, dual-energy computed tomography (DECT), and MRI perfusion are currently under evaluation.
A typical cardiopulmonary exercise testing pattern suggestive of CTEPD includes low PETCO2, high VE/VCO2, low VO2/HR, and low peak VO2.

Comprehensive work-up is performed after 3 months of therapeutic anticoagulation, or sooner in unstable or rapidly deteriorating patients. Ideally CTPA, DSA, and RHC are performed in CTEPH centres, but they are sometimes performed in PH centres depending on the country and organization.
